summary: Reviewed the proposed RBAC/RLS design for splitting event proposal rights from per-event edit rights. The overall direction fits the project role-helper architecture, but the spec leaves several boundary contracts ambiguous enough to produce regressions: full-form create flows still appear venue-pinned, the proposed edit helper leaks creator edit rights to read-only roles, and the RLS update contract may block draft submission or leave related-table writes misaligned.
appears_sound: The spec correctly moves event editing from a broad role+venue capability to a contextual event capability in `src/lib/roles.ts`, matching the project convention that capability functions live in that module.

Reusing `events.manager_responsible_id` as the per-event authority fits the existing schema from `supabase/migrations/20260416210000_manager_responsible_fk.sql:7` and avoids introducing a parallel assignment model.
The proposed SELECT RLS keeps `deleted_at IS NULL` while simplifying global read access for `administrator`, `executive`, and `office_worker`, which matches the stated visibility rule.
The spec correctly identifies that app guards and RLS changes must ship together, reducing the chance of the app allowing actions the database rejects.

findings.description: The spec changes the create-path permission to `canProposeEvents`, but does not fully unwind the existing full-event venue pinning. As written, office_workers without a venue, or office_workers proposing for another venue, can still be blocked by current UI/action data flow even though the proposed rules say full event submission is allowed for any venue.
findings.evidence: `src/actions/events.ts:1050` derives create `venueIds` as `user.role === "office_worker" ? (user.venueId ? [user.venueId] : [])`, and `src/app/events/new/page.tsx:50` filters `availableVenues` to the user's venue.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The broad instruction to split create and update paths may be intended to cover these blocks, but the spec only explicitly removes venue filtering on `/events/propose`.
findings.what_would_confirm: Revise the spec to list the exact venue-selection changes for `saveEventDraftAction`, `submitEventForReviewAction`, and `src/app/events/new/page.tsx`, with tests for office_worker no-venue and cross-venue full submissions.

findings.description: The proposed `canEditEvent` grants creator draft edit rights before checking that the role is editable, so an `executive` who is `createdBy` on a draft or needs-revisions event can edit. That contradicts the proposed rules table where executives remain read-only.
findings.evidence: The helper returns `true` for `event.createdBy === userId` and draft/needs_revisions before `if (role !== "office_worker") return false`, while the table says `Executive` edit is `No` at `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-18-office-worker-propose-and-edit-scope-design.md:36`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Executives may not be able to create events through current UI, but legacy/imported rows or future call-sites could still have an executive as `created_by`.
findings.what_would_confirm: Move the creator self-service clause under an explicit editable-role check, or document that creator draft edit rights intentionally apply to non-office_worker roles too.

findings.description: The proposed UPDATE `WITH CHECK` requires creator-owned rows to remain in `draft` or `needs_revisions`, which can block a creator from submitting a draft into a review status. Because `WITH CHECK` evaluates the new row, a self-service submit transition may fail unless that transition bypasses RLS via admin client or RPC.
findings.evidence: The proposed policy uses `OR (auth.uid() = created_by AND status IN ('draft', 'needs_revisions'))` in `WITH CHECK`, and `submitEventForReviewAction` is an affected update entry point in `src/actions/events.ts:1027`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The submit transition may already use a service-role client or RPC path not shown in the pack, which would bypass this RLS check.
findings.what_would_confirm: Define the allowed status transitions in the RLS policy or explicitly state which submit paths use admin/RPC and add a test for creator draft-to-review submission after the migration.

findings.description: The spec acknowledges secondary-table policies that reference `events`, but leaves their outcomes to a future PR review instead of specifying the resulting contract. That can leave the main event row protected while related rows such as `event_artists`, `event_versions`, approvals, or planning records remain writable through older venue-scoped policies.
findings.evidence: The spec lists secondary tables and provides only a `pg_policies` discovery script at `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-18-office-worker-propose-and-edit-scope-design.md:167`, while the pack only shows the event policy and one debrief policy update.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Those policies may already align with `manager_responsible_id`, as the debrief insert policy in `supabase/migrations/20260416210000_manager_responsible_fk.sql:17` suggests for one table.
findings.what_would_confirm: Include the `pg_policies` output in the spec and record a table-by-table decision for each policy that references `events`.

findings.description: Using a service-role prefetch before `canEditEvent` bypasses the SELECT policy's `deleted_at IS NULL` boundary, but the proposed helper context and UPDATE policy do not include deleted-state checks. This shifts deleted-row protection from RLS to each action without specifying the new responsibility.
findings.evidence: The spec requires service-role fetches to avoid RLS masking at `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-18-office-worker-propose-and-edit-scope-design.md:106`, while the proposed SELECT policy filters `deleted_at IS NULL` and `EventEditContext` contains no `deletedAt` field.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Individual update/delete actions may already reject deleted rows in code not included in the pack.
findings.what_would_confirm: Require the shared event-authz fetch to include `.is('deleted_at', null)` or add deleted state to the edit context and tests for soft-deleted events.

findings.description: The migration plan for the shared capability interface is internally inconsistent: it first suggests keeping `canManageEvents` as a temporary deprecated re-export, then later says to remove it in the same PR. That ambiguity matters because a temporary alias can allow stale call-sites to compile with the old venue-only semantics.
findings.evidence: The helper section says to keep the old name temporarily at `docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-18-office-worker-propose-and-edit-scope-design.md:44`, while the risks and Definition of Done require removing `canManageEvents` in the same PR.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The later risk mitigation and DoD may be intended to supersede the earlier temporary-reexport sentence.
findings.what_would_confirm: Choose one migration strategy in the spec; for this repo, removing `canManageEvents` and letting TypeScript expose every caller is the safer contract.

empty_categories: No new database schema ownership conflict found; the spec reuses `events.manager_responsible_id` instead of adding a parallel assignment table.
No client/server bundle boundary issue is evident from the pack; the proposed capability logic remains in `src/lib/roles.ts` and is consumed by server pages/actions.
No public API surface change is identified in the pack; the proposed change is internal RBAC/RLS behavior.
No circular dependency risk is visible from the provided files and proposed imports.
